column24:
I understand what a TEE is supposed to do but I don't trust them to keep out rogue employees or even the general public.  The way I see the most insecure thing in the world is the "cloud" where more often than not the majority of the tools and coding libraries used are in a permanent beta state.  Even if a certain tool is not, it most likely has multiple instances of beta software used somewhere in its pipeline.  The releases of software don't catalog that so there is no way for the TEE vendor to see that release 123.47A that came out 4 months ago and relied on Library A, which relied on Library B which relied on Libraries C and D.  And library D was just exploited and phones home and does it unnoticed by security enhanced Intel CPUs which defeats the TEE running an Intel box.

I know that is complicated but it is true.  The easiest way to hack into anything is to write awesome open-source code and give it away!  Tons if it with tons and tons of releases.  New features every day!!!  With nightly builds being released on literally tens of thousands of popular coding libraries, the exploit surface is so huge that addressing this issue by trying to reduce the surface area is pointless.  Best to learn how to respond once exploited.
